sentences.text: So we came--
Yes.
So we came to the-- the gynecologist, and he did not examine me.
He did not ask anything.
He just told me-- told us, you know, I haven't got the time today.
Come tomorrow.
Luckily, we did not tell him our names nor where we live.
And we walked out, and we both had the same impression.
He's going to set a trap.
So we came home.
And a few days later, my brother-in-law, who was then, you know, officially working.
He was-- that is what I wanted to tell you, very important-- as an officer in the army.
He was a member of the underground.
He was paid by the underground his officer wages.
He-- he-- they got him a job, you know.
And he was this.
And my brother, who was traveling in the German trains--
Henry.
Henry.
He was taking very often different papers, you know, from one group to another.
He was bringing the literature underground and this.
Was like a messenger.
Not officially, but just to-- just to give them a hand.
Anyway, a few days later, my brother-in-law comes with another name, this time a woman, and we go to there-- to her.
This is another gynecologist.
Yes.
A woman.
And she examines me, and she tells me, look, you are over four months pregnant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Then I sat up and I started to cry.
What I have to tell her, my papers show that I'm not married.
In those times, an unmarried woman was not so often having a child.
You know?
And I started to cry.
And she told me, look, I am not going to touch you.
You are too small.
You are too weak.
And you are too far gone.
You are going to-- to die under my knife.
So I have to tell her a story, yes?
So I told her, look, the father of the child, I don't know where he is.
He was taken by the Germans somewhere.
And I have to work to earn a living.
So she told me, look, I am not going to touch you.
But she gave me an offer, a very decent offer.
She told me, I am going to take you to my clinic, and you will stay with me till the child will be born.
And then we will decide what we are going to do.
She did not-- she did not know that I am Jewish.
She-- probably she did not suspect.
She did not think, perhaps, about-- she saw it as a doctor thing, yes?
She wanted to help.
But of course, I did not take her offer up because I went back home.
My-- my sister, I-- I think that she will die when she heard that this is nothing to do about it.
But that was the least of our worries because two days later, my brother-in-law comes home very agitated.
And he told us that a student of his recognize him on the street and started to blackmail him.
As what?
As a Jew, as a police officer.
A-- you-- that was two things enough to hang him.
[LAUGHS]
So he started to blackmail him.
So he knew that it is time to move somewhere.
So he went to his headquarters, and he told them.
He-- apparently he did not tell them that he is a Jew.
But I don't know if they knew or not.
Anyway, they found for him a place in Warsaw for him and his family.
In Warsaw was this family, which will be afterwards my family.
It was a family, and they were all-- the whole family-- it's a big family.
There were sisters and cousins and brothers and all.
A Polish family, what it is.
A Polish Christian family.
Of course.
Polish Christian family.
And they, you know, had one or another connection with the underground.
Some-- the younger ones were officially members of the underground.
The elder were sympathetic, and they had always literature, the-- the leaflets and the instructions and-- and everything was there.
So they were very flattered because he, the man of the house, was only a sergeant, and suddenly he will get a higher ranking officer.
You-- I think that in Hungary was the same thing, that the ranking in Polish army was very, very-- it is important.
So they were very flattered that they are getting those people in their home.
And they stayed there.
I was supposed to join them.
But at the moment, there was no room there.
So I stayed with another couple, an older couple.
In Warsaw?
In Lvov.
I moved from their flat, and I moved to this older couple which my brother found.
And-- and they were so happy.
They lost their son.
They had one son.
They were-- they did not lose him, they were-- he was taken to Germany-- this-- for forced labor.
But they were so happy that they have someone.
And when she saw me, that I am pregnant, she was so happy that I will have the baby there.
And she will-- you know, a woman of that age.
She were-- she will-- will have something to do, and-- But two months later, then-- by then I was approaching seven months, six month.
Now my brother come-- came to me and told me that there's room in Warsaw, and somebody will come and put me on the train to-- to Warsaw.
Early one day, I am-- I boarded the train.
And I was sitting-- a few women.
Polish, of course.
A young man, Polish.
Polish Women.
Sitting, and I started to talk with them.
You know, this is always better to have some friends and-- friends, but somebody around you.
And suddenly, I could smell-- we were traveling an hour or so, perhaps more-- I could smell burning.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I did not realize yet what it is.
But one of those woman told, oh, they burn again the Jews.
We were not far from Belzec.
And you could smell the burnt flesh all around.
But that was not what angered me yet.
But the others started to laugh.
And they told, oh, we will have-- next week, we will have our ration of soap.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And that opened my eyes.
I knew how anti-Semitic they are.
But that was the end of everything.
How could you use a soap knowing that it comes from a human body?
What was your reaction?
Nothing.
How could I react?
I was sitting there telling nothing.
Then we came to the next stop, was a small town, Rawa-Ruska, where we had to get off the train and to change the trains to take the main one to Warsaw.
I kept always company with those people.
It was no good to be alone, so I kept company with those people.
And the railway station, the waiting room was full of SS men, of Gestapo.
They were looking at every identity card.
Everybody's identity card.
The-- one of them approached us.
And he looked at everybody, was all right.
Suddenly he takes mine, and something struck him.
And he started to ask me questions.
So I told him, I don't speak German.
I don't understand German.
So I was lucky that one of those women spoke a little bit Polish.
People don't spoke-- did not speak much German, but she did.
So he asked me my name, which I told him early.
And then he asked me the date of my birth.
And I got stuck.
I forgot.
I just did not know what to tell.
But my mind raced.
I can't hesitate.
See?
So I told first date which came into my head.
I knew that it has somewhere-- to be somewhere about July, June, August.
Somewhere around this.
So I told the first date which came to me.
And this woman translated.
And I knew that she translated well.
But the consternation on his face.
He looked at my card.
He looked at this woman.
He looked at me.
And he did not know if I was wrong-- he did not speak Polish-- if I was wrong, or she did not translate it properly.
So that moment of hesitation, I opened my coat that he could see that I'm pregnant.
[LAUGHS]
See?
And so he looked at me again and he folded the card and gave me back.
I was dying to have a look.
I knew that I made a mistake somewhere, but I did not know where and what.
I was dying to see it, but I could not do it in front of the other.
The first time when I could do it, it was when I was in Warsaw in my home.
And then I saw I was mistaken by two months.
[LAUGHS] But you see, I was lucky that he did not speak Polish and she did not speak well German.
So he-- he could not make it.
And he was very young.
And he could not make up who was right and who was wrong.
Anyway, we arrived in Warsaw.
I arrived in Warsaw, like my brother told me on this and this eastern station to come out.
He will wait on me-- on me.
And that was the Monday Easter-- Easter Monday, you tell English.
Easter Monday, 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I came out.
There were thousands of people coming in, coming out.
How could you find someone?
So I was looking for a short while around.
I could not find him.
And I would not stop because I just do this.
So I started to walk.
I knew the-- my direction, he-- I knew the address.
But he described me-- I know-- I was never in Warsaw before.
So he described me the-- mine direction which I have to take to find it.
So I went.
Suddenly, I saw a group of people standing around something, laughing and screaming and laughing and-- so I forgot about my fear, and the curiosity took over.
And I approached the group.
And in no time, I found myself in front.
And that, I will never forget.
They were standing around a sewer outlet.
And there on the bars of the sewer were two hands.
Two sets of bones covered with white skin.
Nothing else.
Remember that that were the last days of the ghetto uprising.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were standing around-- children, women-- with sticks and stones, hitting those hands.
Jew, Jew.
I was just-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm telling you, I will never forget that.
You are telling me that I remember.
There was things that you can't erase from your mind.
So I left and I started to walk again.
Of course, I did not dare to ask anybody where I have to go, where is the street.
Because once you started to ask, you are suspicious.
It means you are stranger here.
You-- you are suspicious.
And there were many strangers then trying to get out of the ghetto and things.
So I found the place.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everybody was at home.
My brother was at home.
Of course, in this millions of people around, he could not see me.
And he was distressed.
He did not know if I were fine.
But I found the place.
I come there.
There was the people, Mr. and Mrs.
Prowiszak.
They had two small rooms and a kitchen on the fourth floor-- fourth floor in the very old part of Warsaw.
So old that the toilet was in the yard.
You had to go the four floors down and up every time.
The place was full of people.
Really full.
There was the two hosts.
There's my sister, my brother-in-law, their daughter, a cousin, Niunka, some few other people, and my brother.
It was crowded.
And I walked in, it was-- it is.
So in meantime, we had to find a story, a new story how come that they are so interested in me, and how come that I have a child under my maiden name.
So my brother-in-law got a story together that my husband was an officer in his regiment, and we were very friendly.
But as the-- first the Russian and then the Gestapo was looking for my husband for some other thing.
I don't know.
They were looking anyway for every officer.
So-- so I reverted to my maiden name, not to have his name.
I reverted to my maiden name.
And in meantime, I found out that I am pregnant.
And that was the story.
And they swallowed it.
You see?
It was very-- very-- it is plausible story for those times.
But it was so crowded that I could not stay there.
But the cousin, Niunka, had a room in the next street.
It was on the fifth floor.
And she gave the room to my disposal, because she was a nurse in the hospital and she was more in the hospital than at home.
So I went there alone.
But that room was overlooking the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were the last days.
I heard the burning.
I saw the burning.
I heard the screams.
I heard the shooting.
I saw the planes circle.
I saw the Gestapo going in, all armed to their teeth.
And I was sitting there, and it was-- I was not inside, but I-- I was outside.
But I went through the-- the-- the-- the last days.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, a few days passed.
Suddenly, my brother comes in.
And he tells me, come with me and don't take even the toothbrush with you.
Just as you stand.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I went out with him, and what happened?
Niunka, the cousin, went into some trouble.
She wanted to help some-- a couple, a Jewish couple.
And she went into trouble, and the Gestapo was-- so-- looking for her.
So my brother knew that the first thing they will come to her room.
So he got me out.
But as it was no room in where I should have stayed, he put me on a train to Krakow.
As I was standing.
I'm telling you.
I had my coat on.
I was over seven months pregnant.
And I go to Krakow, where I was not in my life.
You know, that is like that.
Przemysl is here.
Lwow is here.
I went first to Lwow, then I was up to Warsaw, then I was down to Krakow.
All over Poland.
And that was my '40 [POLISH], which I didn't know that it is '40 yet.
And pregnant.
Then it was coming to summer.
You see?
I come in-- it is April, into spring.
In May-- in May, I was in Krakow.
May is a beautiful month in Poland.
It is the full spring.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I come to Krakow to this family.
That was some family of those Polish couple, Prowiszaks, from Warsaw.
That was a woman and two daughters.
One of the daughters was around my age, it is presumed my age.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No.
No, no.
No, I--
No.
Unsuspected.
Even not suspected.
I will tell you a thing like that.
I was traveling from Warsaw to Krakow.
In the compartment, there were two women and a man, a young man.
And I was talking with those women, it-- but after the war, my husband showed to his friend-- a Polish friend-- my photo.
That one which I have in the desk, which is so-- so--
Creased.
Yes.
Damaged.
He had it on.
And this man looked at him and  told him, that's not possible.
She can't be Jewish.
I was traveling with her in the same compartment from Warsaw to Krakow.
You see?
That is how nobody ever had an inkling that I could be Jewish.
With my black hair and with my black eyes, you know?
The main thing is that I spoke perfect-- German.
--Polish.
Not German.
Polish.
The Polish language is very hard.
Most Jews had a sing-song or they had-- if they were, for example, from a family where they spoke Yiddish, they had always an accent.
And-- and my Polish was not tainted.
You see?
Now, anyway, I come to Krakow.
And next day, she has as a-- an idea, the young one She wants to go to the pictures.
I can't tell her no, that I am afraid.
See, I have to go.
So I went with her to the pictures.
We had pictures.
I don't remember the picture, a German one.
And we came out.
And we were walking, because it was a beautiful day.
And suddenly, I see a familiar face.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
It was the son of our janitor in Przemysl.
He recognized me, of course, because they were-- as long as I remember, they were there, living at the house.
And he asked me-- he did not tell in front of her anything.
So she has-- he asked me to come with him.
Then, what I had to expect?
I expected him or to call a policeman or a German or to ask me for money.
In the best case.
So-- and must be my husband.
In the best case.
So I was standing there, waiting for him to speak.
So he looks at me and he told-- tells me like that.
I am so happy that I see you.
And I am so happy just to see that you look so fine being pregnant.
And if you need something, you know where to find me, and I will be always ready to help.
And I am going now back to Przemysl, and I will try to reach your parents in the ghetto and tell them that I saw you.
And very decent young man.
Ukrainian.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I'm staying in this Warsaw-- in this Krakow place.
And this woman was not very happy with me.
It means she started to panic.
I was very high then-- by then.
And she started to panic that I am going to have a baby in her place.
And she was ringing every day to-- to Warsaw.
Take her back.
I don't want the baby here.
I don't want the complication here.
And that, I was end of the seven months.
I had no roof over my head.
She did not want me in her place.
In Warsaw, there was no room for me.
And I was sitting there.
Then in two weeks time, everything-- this, they told me to come.
Come back to Warsaw, and I did.
And I stayed with the-- the whole family.
It means my sister was there, my brother-in-law, and their daughter.
And the-- the-- the couple, the Polish couple.
Their name was Paula and Frank Prowiszak.
And as we will talk later, I will call her Aunt Paula, and him Uncle Frank.
Because she introduced me to everybody as her niece, a daughter of her brother.
First, it was because she wanted to cover us as the resistant people.
You know, the resistance people.
And after a while, she just loved me.
You know?
We just went on so well.
She was like a mother to me.
And she told to me-- not-- not once, but very often.
She lost two sons.
She know-- one son in defending Warsaw.
And one son who tried-- why-- why my story was so plausible, because her son was an officer, the other son, in the army.
And he was in danger because the Gestapo was looking for him.
So he changed his name as well.
And as he changed his name, he was unlucky.
He was round up on the street of Warsaw and sent to Auschwitz.
As a Pole.
So she lost them both.
And he-- she told me, look, I was praying my whole life for a daughter, and God listened to me and sent me you.
She never suspected you were Jewish?
No.
Oh, I wore a cross.
From time to time.
Not often because as religious as she was, she warned me not to go to the church-- to-- to confession, because you never know if you can confide in the-- in this priest.
You know?
They knew that there were many priests on this Gestapo pay.
And so she did not go to confession, and she didn't allow me to go to confession, for-- which I was very glad, yes.
But from time to time, I went to the church.
The-- anyway, I stayed there.
And again, we tried to normalize a little bit the life.
In two weeks time, I started to give birth.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lost water in the morning, and she decided that I will give birth at home.
I must tell here that Aunt Paula-- as I told, I will call her Aunt Paula.
You will know that it is this Polish woman.
Aunt Paula was a midwife.
She was one of the best midwives in Warsaw.
She was known everywhere, in every hospital.
And-- and she decided that as I am so young and everything is all right, I will give birth at home.
After one day, she saw that something is wrong.
So she rang the women's hospital, and they accepted me.
It was a very rare thing because the hospitals were full, and you could not-- but the matron of the hospital was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, school friend of Aunt Paula.
So she found for me room there.
I will not tell you the whole story because it is too long.
I was in labor two days and two nights.
Nothing happens.
I was absolutely-- everybody was panicked-- panicking, and that.
On the night of the second-- second night, it started to get everything out.
The baby is coming.
By then, everybody was around me.
The head doctor and all the doctors and all the nurses.
It was middle of the night.
They had nothing else to do.
Everybody was standing there.
The difficult birth.
And I needed the last push when suddenly I saw the door open, and the nurse is coming in.
She had one look at me, and she tells, what she's doing here, she's Jewish?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And everything stopped.
I could not push.
I could not-- I just felt faint.
And they were all standing around me, and nobody understood what happened with me.
You know?
And then-- today, after 50-odd years, when I think about this moment, I can't understand why the others did-- did not react when she told it.
They did not hear?
I heard it on my bed.
But then I-- I don't know if it was my imagination, or my-- the-- the fear or something.
But I can see her and hear her voice even now.
But anyway, they had to-- again?
All right.
Can I talk?
Anyway, they had to get me-- tell-- help me.
You know?
The-- they started-- the doctors and nurses, there were no-- they were no help like today, with all the medical-- medications.
They did not have even an aspirin there in the hospital-- aspirin.
But the child was born prematurely in eight months.
And I started to hemorrhage.
And the doctor told-- the-- that was the last thing I heard.
Take her to the operating room.
And--
